question: How do I fund a testnet account (and a contract address) with XLM using Friendbot?

golden.answer: For Testnet, call `https://friendbot.stellar.org/?addr=<G... or C...>` (GET or POST per the current service docs) to create/fund a new test account with **10,000 fake XLM**. Futurenet has its own Friendbot host, and local Quickstart can expose a local faucet. Friendbot never funds Mainnet, is rate-limited, and test networks reset. Treat HTTP status as the contract: success is 200, while invalid/already-funded requests return 400; do not pin the success response body because it is intentionally unstable.
keyFacts: Names Testnet/Futurenet and G/C-address support and the fake 10,000-XLM behavior.
Distinguishes status/error behavior from an unstable success payload.
avoid: Do NOT claim Mainnet funding or a permanent exact rate limit.
Do NOT require a stable success JSON schema.
golden.notes:
Friendbot is testnet-only; claiming mainnet funding is the trap. Verified: REST GET|POST / with ?addr=; funds G... and (when fund_contract_addresses enabled) C...; ~10,000 fake XLM; Futurenet has its own friendbot-futurenet.stellar.org.
Also good if the answer: Friendbot funds both `G...` account addresses and `C...` contract addresses. | The Stellar Lab also offers a Friendbot funding UI for testnet.
Golden cites: developers.stellar.org friendbot/faucet docs or the stellar/friendbot repo.
GT-49 CORRECTION 2026-07-11: grade network/address/status behavior, not a response-body snapshot.

corroboration.claim: Friendbot supports Testnet/Futurenet G and C addresses with GET/POST addr; Testnet creates a new account with 10,000 fake XLM, while success-body shape is intentionally unstable and invalid/already-funded requests are HTTP 400.

verified.by: Golden-truth sweep import — The core Testnet funding flow was correct but under-specified current G/C/Futurenet support and treated the success payload as stable.
evidence: Solo scratchpad 575 GT-49 primary 3335 and blind 3341; no valid funding side effect was performed.
rootCause: Service-surface freshness and an unjustified stable-response assumption.
